title: Adiabatic Ramp Dynamics Across the ETH--MBL Transition in Disordered XXZ Spin Chain
abstract: Many-body localization(MBL) provides a mechanism by which isolated interacting quantum systems with disorder can avoid thermalization unlike ergodic systems satisfying the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis(ETH). Many-body localized systems retain signatures of their initial conditions at long times, whereas systems obeying ETH lose such information as they approach thermal equilibrium. Studying Non-equilibrium dynamics across ETH-MBL crossover is an important problem in condensed matter physics. Adiabatic control of parameters in interacting disordered systems provides a powerful framework to investigate MBL phases and their dynamical robustness. Using exact diagonalization and time-dependent numerical methods we study the effects of adiabatically ramped interactions in a disordered spin-1/2 XXZ chain, a paradigmatic model for exploring the many-body localization transition. By monitoring diagonal entropy density and entanglement entropy density growth across various ramp speeds, and system sizes. Our study incorporates Finite-size effects of spectral observables to probe the transition between ergodic and localized phases. The numerical results show that localized dynamical behavior remains largely intact under sufficiently slow ramp evolution, while increasing the driving rate promotes stronger excitation generation and larger entropy growth. This trend highlights the strong dependence of nonequilibrium adiabatic dynamics in disordered interacting quantum many-body systems.
summary: This work examines how non-equilibrium dynamics differ between ergodic (ETH) and Many-Body Localized (MBL) quantum phases in a disordered spin chain. By adiabatically ramping the system parameters, the authors use entropy measures to show that the rate of excitation generation is strongly suppressed in the localized phase compared to the thermalizing phase. This confirms that entropy production serves as a powerful diagnostic tool for probing quantum thermalization dynamics.
main_problem: The study investigates non-equilibrium dynamics across the transition between ergodic (ETH) and Many-Body Localized (MBL) quantum phases.
main_result: The entropy production (diagonal and entanglement) is significantly higher and more dependent on the driving rate in the ergodic phase compared to the MBL phase, confirming the dynamical distinction between the two regimes.
method: The authors employed exact diagonalization and real-time numerical evolution to simulate the system's time evolution under time-dependent Hamiltonian ramps.
model_or_system: The model is a disordered spin-1/2 XXZ chain, described by a Hamiltonian with disorder terms ($h_j$) and a time-dependent interaction coupling ($J_{zz}(t)$). The system is analyzed in the zero-magnetization sector.
key_observables: Diagonal entropy density, entanglement entropy density, and the adjacent-gap ratio (used to characterize static spectral statistics).
important_parameters: Disorder strength ($h_0$), system size ($L \le 14$), and the ramp speed ($v$) characterizing the adiabaticity of the time-dependent drive.
main_assumptions: The analysis is limited by finite-size effects, though the results show robustness across multiple system sizes. The study assumes the validity of the ETH/MBL framework for characterizing thermalization.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the disorder-averaged adjacent-gap ratio transitioning from GOE (ergodic) to Poisson (localized) statistics, and show entropy measures ($	ext{sd}, 	ext{sent}$) varying with ramp speed for different protocols.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the static ETH-MBL crossover using spectral statistics. It then analyzes the non-equilibrium dynamics by applying three different time-dependent driving protocols (linear, quadratic, exponential) and comparing the resulting entropy growth in the ergodic versus localized regimes.
why_interesting: The use of entropy measures to diagnose non-equilibrium dynamics across a fundamental quantum phase transition (ETH-MBL) is highly relevant for understanding how quantum information spreads or is suppressed in interacting many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Disorder-averaged adjacent-gap ratio r versus disor- der strength for system sizes L = 10, 12, and 14.

FIG. 2. Dependence of the diagonal entropy density, sd(tf), on the normalized ramp rate, v/Jzz(0), for the disordered XXZ spin chain. Panels in the left and right columns correspond to the ETH and MBL regimes, respectively. The rows represent linear, quadratic, and exponential ramp protocols from top to bottom. Results are shown for system sizes L = 10, 12, and 14 to illustrate finite-size dependence and the influence of different driving protocols on entropy generation during nonequilibrium dynamics.

FIG. 3. Behavior of the entanglement entropy density, sent(tf), with normalized ramp rate, v/Jzz(0), in the disordered XXZ chain. Left and right columns show results in the ETH and MBL regimes, respectively, while the rows correspond to linear, quadratic, and exponential driving protocols. Data for system sizes L = 10, 12, and 14 are included to examine how system size and ramp protocol affect entanglement growth generation during nonequilibrium evolution.
FIG. 4. Diagonal entropy density sd(tf) and entanglement entropy density sent(tf) for ETH and MBL phase. Panel (a)-(c) for L = 10, L = 12 and L = 14.
